articles.title: OpenCode User Guide
articles.excerpt: OpenCode is a powerful AI-assisted CLI programming tool. This article introduces its core features and usage.
content: OpenCode is an AI-assisted programming tool that runs in the terminal. It understands your natural language instructions and helps you with code writing, refactoring, debugging, documentation generation, and other software engineering tasks.
After installation, run `opencode` in your terminal to start.
Run the following in your project directory:
Once in the conversation interface, you can describe your needs in natural language. OpenCode will automatically analyze your codebase and perform the requested actions.
OpenCode can precisely modify file contents, supporting:
No need to open files manually. OpenCode supports:
Manage version control directly in conversation:
When you need to look up information, OpenCode can:
Complex tasks can be broken into multiple steps:
Keep your context clear when conversing with OpenCode. Start a new task after finishing the current one to avoid context mixing.
- OpenCode does not automatically commit code; you need to explicitly request it
- Sensitive information (keys, passwords) is never uploaded

OpenCode deeply integrates AI capabilities into your development workflow, allowing you to focus more on problem-solving and solution design. Whether for daily development or learning new technologies, it is a powerful assistant.

articles.title: JSLY Blog User Guide
articles.excerpt: Welcome to JSLY's Blog! This guide introduces every feature of the blog to help you get started quickly and make the most of it.
content: JSLY's Blog is a static blog built on GitHub Pages, using Vanilla HTML/CSS/JS with no build tools required. It offers a smooth reading experience, powerful search, and various personalization settings. This guide walks you through each feature.
The homepage consists of the following sections:
The top of the page displays the blog title and a welcome message. Click the "Start Reading" button to jump to the article list.

The fixed top navigation bar contains links to Home, Articles, Public Submissions, and About. Click to smoothly scroll to the corresponding section. The current section's nav item is highlighted. On mobile, the nav bar collapses into a hamburger menu.
Useful icons on the right side of the nav bar:
- **Dark Mode Toggle** — Switch between light and dark themes with one click
- **Settings Icon** — Open reading settings to change article viewing mode
- **Menu Button** — Expand navigation links on mobile

The "Latest Articles" section displays all articles in a card grid. Each card contains the title, tags, excerpt, publication date, estimated reading time, and view count. Cards float upward with a shadow effect on hover.
The "About Me" section shows the blogger's profile card with social links and statistics. The card automatically switches to a dark theme in dark mode.
The footer includes social media links (QQ, GitHub, Luogu, Qzone, AtCoder), quick navigation links, and contact information.
Click any article card to view the article details. If the article has a cover image, it will be displayed at the top of the card; cards without a cover image use a gradient placeholder.
Click the gear icon in the navigation bar to open settings and switch between two viewing modes:
Articles open in a modal dialog overlay on the current page. Features include reading progress bar, ESC to close, click-outside to close, and scroll-linked TOC highlighting.
Articles open in a new tab (`/article/{id}`), suitable for reading multiple articles or keeping the homepage intact. A "Back to Articles" link is provided at the top.
Your preference is saved via Cookie and persists across browser sessions.
Click the search icon in the navigation bar to open the search dialog. Two search methods are supported:
Enter keywords to match article titles, tags, excerpts, and full content in real-time. Matching keywords are highlighted.
The pinyin-pro library is integrated for pinyin-based search. For example, searching "zhinan" will match articles with "指南" in the title. Pinyin search requires at least 2 characters.
In the search dialog, use ↑/↓ to navigate results, Enter to open the selected article, and ESC to close.
Once an article is open, you can use the following features:
A table of contents sidebar appears on the left, auto-generated from H1/H2/H3 headings. The current section is highlighted as you scroll. Click a TOC item to jump smoothly.
A share button is at the bottom of each article. On supported browsers, it invokes the native share dialog; otherwise, it copies the link to the clipboard.

The Giscus comment system (based on GitHub Discussions) is integrated at the bottom of each article. Log in with your GitHub account to comment and reply. The comment theme follows dark mode automatically.

Click the moon/sun icon to toggle. The nav bar, article cards, code highlighting, profile card, and comments all adapt automatically. Your preference is saved in localStorage, and the system theme is detected on first visit.
Two types of deep links are supported:
- **Hash Links**: `index.html#article-N` — opens the article modal directly on the homepage
- **Page Links**: `/article/{id}` — displays the full article on a standalone page

If the specified article ID does not exist, the page automatically shows a 404. Both link types are compatible. Hash links in page mode will redirect to the homepage and open the modal. For sharing/bookmarking, use the `/article/{id}` format.
There is a dedicated [Public Submissions](/public/) section where community members can contribute articles.
Click "Public Submissions" in the navigation bar, or visit `/public/` directly. The page has its own Hero section and article list, sharing the same UI style as the main blog.
- **GitHub Issue** (Recommended) — Click the "Submit via GitHub" button, fill in the template, and submit. Your GitHub username will be automatically used as the author name.
- **Email** — Send an email to submit@blog.jsly.asia with the article title, body (Markdown), and your name.
Submissions are reviewed before publication. Approved articles will appear in the Public Submissions section.

Public articles also support Modal Mode and New Page Mode, with table of contents, code highlighting, comments (Giscus), and sharing. Author badges are displayed on cards to distinguish submitted articles from main blog articles.

Although built on a simple static architecture, JSLY's Blog provides a complete and comfortable reading experience. The full source code is open source on GitHub (AGPL-3.0). If you have questions or suggestions, feel free to reach out via the footer contact info or article comments.
